headline: Lost Piece of American History Found in London Archive
meta_description: A volunteer sorting historical papers at London's National Archives has uncovered a rare 1776 Declaration of Independence—the only known copy outside the US.
summary_short: While cataloguing Royal Navy correspondence, a UK volunteer discovered one of just 11 surviving copies of the 1776 Declaration of Independence.
body:
A remarkable find has emerged from the quiet work of archival research: a volunteer sorting historical letters at The National Archives in Kew has discovered a rare 1776 printing of the US Declaration of Independence—one of only 11 known copies to survive, and the only one ever found outside the United States.

Michael Scurr was cataloguing correspondence for the America 250 – Royal Navy Admirals' and Captains' letters project when he spotted a reference to seized documents in a January 1777 letter from Captain Thomas Fitzherbert of HMS Raisonable. Closer inspection revealed something extraordinary: an "Exeter Declaration," printed in New Hampshire between July 16-19, 1776, by Robert Luist Fowle for the New Hampshire Gazette.

The document's journey tells a story of wartime procedure and historical accident. It came from the Dalton of Newburyport, a private American vessel captured by the Raisonable on December 24, 1776. Naval protocol required all seized papers to be presented to a court—yet Fitzherbert forwarded the Declaration to London instead, where it remained for 250 years, eventually reaching The National Archives.

The discovery arrives as the institution prepares exhibitions marking America's 250th anniversary, making this forgotten piece of founding history all the more timely. Sometimes the most significant discoveries hide quietly in plain sight.
